rating 4.0. Very pretty, soft shimmery satin appearance NOT metallic [[VIDEOID:8f3b25ebd726f8d4e1bb0245945def7a]] Beautiful color, nice satiny shimmer with consistent finish and the typical texture. Perfect weight for many applications. The color/finish is NOT metallic, I wouldn't call it any level of shine it's a soft shimmer that looks matt when not angled under light. Cuts well with my Brother ScanNCut machine, scissors, my worklion paper cutter, and metallic/paint pens, regular pens/pencils, markers/sharpies write on it and dry on it well. It also dries with a normal time with my ink jet Canon Pixma ix6820 printer. I will be using it for business cards and polymer clay jewelry backing cards.

rating 3.0. Mine arrived dried out [[VIDEOID:caba306d996e489965e5d06bdcadc67e]] I am unsure if my tube was exposed to freezing temperatures during transit, or was very old, or exposed to heat, but mine wouldn't express when I got it, I took the tip off, cleaned with warm water, got rid of some semi solid glue in a ring around the cap base, but there wasn't a plug in the tip and with great effort some dry chunky cottage cheese-like glue comes out.

I've heard only good things so I know this isn't normal, but there are no returns for this product so I don't think I can go through amazon for it unfortunately.

I still included it in my testing/comparison experiment against art glitter glue, bearly art glue, and super glue gel on cardstock/brushed metal finish paper/glitter paper.

Surprisingly, this glue still had the second fastest holding/tacky speed and was strong when it fully dried. The bearly art glue was the best, the art glitter glue was strong but allowed for too much play and took 4x as long to set as the others. I'm not sure why I'm allowed to return the art glitter glue and not this, but I want to give it a second chance I just don't know where to buy it in person.

The two ways you can use it- as wet glue for permanent fix or application for removable tacky attachments is something that sets it a part from many other adhesives and is why I still want to include it in my arsenal.

Update: a few weeks later I took a risk and got another one via subscribe and save. This one was perfect and smooth out of the package and it's a great glue. It's just a shame I can't return and get a refund for the useless first one.

rating 5.0. Nice size, nice grid, not shiny I got this to protect my work bench where I do art work (polymer clay, mixed media, vinyl, paper, painting, jewelry making, resin) to protect the wood top.

This arrived fairly quickly, (there was a delay on UPS's side), and it arrived flat in a large box. It has a strong smell (like of a new plastic shower curtain) but dissipates eventually. I am trying different things on the underside to prevent sliding, right now I'm using tacky dots. I don't know why there isn't an option for this type of cutting mat to have a non-SLIP backing. I ordered the clear and its translucent grey and looks nice. It's a nice thickness and edges aren't too sharp. These are VERY pricey in craft stores so I'm happy I found this one.

I also ordered Creative Grids CGRMAT2436 mat, it hasn't arrived yet, but I'm going to compare it to this one and decide if I'll keep it for the other half of my work bench or return one of them and get a second of my favorite.

For those that record video of their endeavors, even with a work light at it's brightest, this only shows up with a sheen, no glare.

rating 4.0. I bought this whole set for one tool! I got a 4 piece kit (the solid black cheap plastic tools pictured) at a craft store a long time ago and boy I use that spear shaped tool all the time. I actually wore/broke the tip off and can't find that set ANYWHERE. I already have versions or the same of every other tool included so I'll give away/re-home most of them, but some of the blue silicon tips are slightly different from what I have and I like the feel of the handles of the small ball tools better than the wooden handled ones I currently have so it was overall a good upgrade.

rating 5.0. Nice quality vinyl, applies easily even with an old crochety iron Great results, backing really sticks good and makes me worried I'm going to pull it right off the shirt, but it works well and holds up to washing well so far.

Nice color selection, PLEASE make available in 12x12 inch sizing!

rating 5.0. Quality vinyl Glitter doesn't shed off, applies fast and easily, nice quality/feel. Will comment on longevity once I start to see stress/wear.

The colors are nice, PLEASE make these in 12x12 inch!

rating 5.0. Works excellent with decorative and indoor/outdoor quality vinyl Works excellently and great price/value for craft/auto use. I use it with cricut and off brand decorative vinyls, with oracal 631 and 651 vinyl, and on every surface I've tried- glass, metal, plastic, cardstock, etc. I use a cricut maker 20% of the time and a brother scanncut 225 80% of the time whether it's vinyl art being sold or used for my own business, this stuff works well, sticks when it should and peels off when it should.

Easy to use and apply and this roll is going to last a long time.

Highly recommended!

rating 5.0. Perfect replacement for cricut brand, works flawlessly with my cricut maker Great price/deal, works well, same adhesion and longevity as the cricut (obviously not ideal in itself) but this price is more affordable for frequent use. Recommended, no issues feeding/reading.

Update: I've been using for 2 months now and the only difference I've noticed is that when using the little spatula for weeding it visibly scraps the adhesive tack (leaves lines of damage to it and accumulates some gum on the edge of the spatula). I'm still using the same mat and grip seems minimally affected so far so I still recommend and cleans with hand wipes same as the brand name.

rating 3.0. Small but not sturdy, bends/gives, and molding lip affects pattern These are small and easy and light to grasp/hold, but they are flimsy in that with any real pressure they give and so your desired effect is lessened/deviated at times. The molding lip from manufacture leaves a small sharp flap on the tips which affects design unless you try to carefully cut/smooth it off. These would not work on medium to firm clays. It took effort and they all bent when I attempted to use on soft conditioned premo translucent (pictured). They work less well with fimo, cernit, and pardo. The pack is very cheap and comes in a nice sleeve and the variety of tools is nice and I will still find uses for many of them, so they might be worth having for various art forms as long as you don't expect them to be sturdy.

rating 5.0. I might be the only person who switched an atlas for this When I got back into polymer clay I did research and couldn't afford a lucy clay machine but I did spend $80 on an atlas wellness. It's well made but it made horrid ridges right out of the box at every thickness. I can't even use for my cosclay, it sticks to the rollers and it flakes it up and off and puts air inside it horribly.

I read reviews on this one and the non-stick rollers seemed promising. It's the only thing I can use with cosclay or 50%+ cosclay mixes and there are only soft ridges at the thinner settings.

You need to be kind and gentle with the thickness dial (reviews and recommendations agree this is often what breaks first), but if I get the planned 3-5 years out of this I'll be happy.

rating 5.0. Great selection I use these to cover knots in my 1mm magic stretch cord I use for handmade polymer clay Christmas ornaments and hanging decor. It takes a bit of effort to snug the knot enough for the large one to fit since the cord itself has so much memory but they work fantastic and are easy to crimp and look good. Prices are same or cheaper than craft stores and my local stores only carry the silver not the gold. I like the finish differences some just go better with my individual projects than others. Close them with care, it can be easy to squash them a little and not get them to snug up in a smooth closed curve if you're not careful or use an inappropriate tool.
rating 4.0. Great color range, great price Great price for 12x12 of 80 lb thickness with this variety paper color/saturation is good, this will last me a while
rating 4.0. Nice heavy quality paper, vivid colors Smooth texture and nice mix of colors. I needed some browns to make a paper layered nativity scene for a gift and used them up quickly.

rating 2.0. Easy to make a mess, easy to burn If you can get it right it makes an attractive puffy snow effect. But it's drippy and comes out in gobs and gets all over the place in a manner that is not predictable. I've used a craft heat gun, a shop heat gun, and a lighter and it takes a lot of effort to get it to puff but I feel it's way too easy to singe it (it will brown/blacken like a marshmallow while some is still liquid and not puffy throughout where it's burning. I shake it thoroughly and it happens with various thicknesses/amounts. I just don't think the reaction is even.

rating 4.0. Strong and durable I use this for my custom-made polymer clay Christmas ornaments. It is strong, has some give but not overly stretchy, and doesn't stretch out. The only difficulty is getting a knot down nice and tight- you have to keep a lot of tension on it to place a bead crimp on it to hold it secure due to the strong memory of the line.

I really like this product for it's strength and will continue to buy it in this or the extra large roll size. I have seen the glitter gold/silver versions in person and they are mostly translucent with a few flecks so I will be sticking to the black or clear only.

rating 5.0. Much preferred The standard mat for my Brother ScanNCut 125EGY was just too sticky, even after repeated use. It was ripping/pulling vinyl backing off the vinyl, ripping paper and card stock, etc. This one works a lot better but is strong enough for lots of things (indoor/outdoor vinyl, 7mil mylar stencil sheets, 110 lb cardstock.

rating 4.0. Works fine for cutting machines, vinyl This works well for securing items on the mats (I'm using a cricut maker and a brother scanncut sdx125) as well as for applying transfer tape onto vinyl and vinyl onto flat surfaces.

Pros/Cons of rubber vs plastic/acrylic.

It obviously can get dented/gouged, dried out, cracked etc. And it picks up things almost like a lint roller. But it does have some give.

Acrylic is harder to damage and fairly timeless but has no give. I may eventually get one of each but for now this is fine and works well. The metal hardware isn't junky, there's not a super strong smell. The underside of the handle has some pits/bubbles/cosmetic issues but nothing that affects function and the rubber roller itself is smooth.

rating 5.0. Works great, kinder edge These are much needed for me- wider, gentler edge than the dinky, sharp, and incompletely trimmed scraper that came in the tool kit for the cricut maker. They are comfortable to use and do not scratch, the cricut one can scratch out of the box but definitely has a sharper, hard/rigid plastic edge and if you get any defects, it's even more likely.

These were a good price and it works well on decorative vinyl, oracal 651 and 631, and other applications. There is enough give to be kinder during application but it's not flimsy.

rating 4.0. Works well but I might be switching for use on my polymer clay This works really well and cures fast, but over time gets finicky (pulls from edges, doesn't completely flatten. I don't have much issue with bubbles no matter how old it is.

When I got out of clay for several months due to having no time, then pulled out some of my pieces, I was disappointed to see the finish had some pits, fingerprints, and scuffs that were all super noticeable. I can only assume that it wasn't fully cured or was and doesn't have a truly protective resin-quality finish. Considering I cured most of them for at least 15-20 minutes instead of the specified 2-6 minutes, I don't think I under-cured them.

I am trying out EZ Dome YV Resin from FTH Supplies and Deep Shine UV Finish from Tiny Pandora and will update the review with how they compare soon.

rating 5.0. Good quality vinyl Cuts well (I'm using a cricut maker and a brother scanncut SDX125). Well made, backing is nice, several brands of transfer sheets work well. Good color pack, we'll see what colors are most popular and order full rolls.

rating 5.0. Good quality Good quality vinyl, using a cricut maker and a brother scanncut SDX125 to cut for decor (indoor/outdoor uses). The sellers sticker was impossible to get off but seems to have been applied over a boogered up section anyway.

rating 5.0. Nice quality, has a nice shimmer/sheen I got the 24 pack 651 12x12 kit, the 63 color 631 12x12 kit, and a roll of 631 white and 631 black. It is well made, well cut, and with good backing. They cut pretty well with the Brother ScanNCut SDX125 and the Cricut Maker though both struggle initially with cutting depth being off even with half depth use. The Brother improved immensely with a single tweak, the Cricut cuts through everything and drags little pieces no matter the settings.

Results are good, crisp detailed designs work well and application is easy. I've used with cricut transfer sheets, but have switched to a big roll due to cost of YRYMHT transfer tape (12in x 50 ft).

rating 5.0. Absolutely necessary, should come with EVERY ScanNCut! Had to buy this separately for my new Brother ScanNCut SDX125EGY. It arrived quickly and fed/read perfectly and makes scanning and uploading very quick and easy. It has a permanent lift up flap of clear plastic to protect your scanner during scanning. I've seen no finickiness with my machine with any type of mat insertion or recognition.

It picked up some very tiny and thin text from a word document.

Has the newer style hanging hole built in.

rating 5.0. Really nice cardstock [[VIDEOID:98dcf7ddacbbee5980d313343fe5894c]] Sturdy, different weights by color as follows:

Navy, Ruby Red, Midnight Black, Holiday Green, Lemonade, Baby Blue, Candy Pink, all 100 lb
Gold Metallic, Silver Metallic, both 105 lb
Grocery Bag - 65 lbs

The metallics are really lustrous, not glossy but a nice shine. All the papers have a good feel. It arrived in pretty good condition, it is packed in the thinnest plastic ever so one corner was a bit smished.

Both the Cricut Maker and the Brother SDX125 ScanNCut struggled with the thicker ones cutting all the way, it took one simple adjustment for the Brother to correct, the Cricut struggled no matter what pressure and material settings I used.

I'm not really into cards and papercrafts too much but I have used these for business cards, earring and jewelry backings, boxes, and a few cards for friends and family and it's not hard to see how papercrafting can get addictive.

rating 2.0. Finicky dispensing, case comes open/apart without pushing on opening tab Price was OK, it's a pain it doesn't come as an option with different color unit or with 1/4 inch width tape.

For mine, the case doesn't clip closed securely! I can with minimal pressure or bumping it, split it open without depressing the clip for opening. This is really really annoying. I mean I'm glad I don't need to break a nail or use a coin or flathead to get it to open when it's time to replace the roll, but it shouldn't just come apart with use, the clip is POINTLESS! I don't see any cracks or defects that are unique to my unit at all.

It's really finicky with application. 60% of the time it applies the whole width of adhesive, the rest of the time it repeatedly dispenses half or less width. I end up making repeat passes most of the time. The protective tip flip up cover is nice and secure and keeps it clean, if only the rest of the thing snapped so securely.

The grip is comfortable for me (lefty with past hand/wrist injuries) except for a distinct gap in between the two halves which has a slight sharp lip that can be uncomfortable.

When it runs out I'll probably try a lesser known brand, really hoping to find better quality somewhere.

The tape and dots themselves are perfect (when it applies them down!) The stick level is great for all the applications I've tried.

rating 5.0. Cricut Maker & Design Space vs Brother ScanNCut SDX125EGY & Canvas Workspace... fight to the death! [[VIDEOID:e705b2ff8d1d9982f8c503820c4160ea]] I'm not quite sure how I got here. I am a polymer clay, sculpting, sketching, graphic design, and beading dabbler and have never understood the draw of paper crafts, being hunched over cutting out doo-dads, or making fantastical cards that will likely just get tossed out.

And then I simultaneous bought a Cricut Maker and a Brother ScanNCut SDX125EGY. And vinyl. And tools. And foil. And transfer paper, and a scanning mat. And 110 lb weight cardstock. After watching so many youtube channels in research, I came to the conclusion that you have to be super perky, with super white teeth, bright lipstick, wear too many rings, and get too excited by new color palates, stickers, and sentiments to be associated with this stuff. So how did ***I*** get here. I think it started when I googled if you could cut stencils to use ON polymer clay with one of these machines. I found absolutely no proof anyone is/was doing this, but to my shock, I found many references to people using them to cut baked thin sheets of polymer clay itself, and a few, (Gasp), saying they cut unbaked/raw polymer clay with it (why would you gum up an expensive machine like that?!)

I then knew if I was serious I'd have to watch and research tutorials on the software and design process for each of them. So I felt like I was at least partially prepared to go through with this test...

UNBOXING:

I picked up the Cricut in person at Joann's (or as my favorite morning radio show refers to them now- J-Fab). I felt like confetti and loud music was going to fall on me, the employee was so smugly congratulatory and genuinely happy for me, like I had passed through a sacred set of rites and been inducted into a super high echelon society now. She gushed about coming in for help and classes and that I was going to love-love-love it. It was hefty in weight and a length that is a tad bulky but the sturdy box and handle made it easy. The box was pristine. I got it home and opened it up and it was well-packed but with two irksome features- everything wrapped in plastic and the mats had been curved and wrapped around and under and were totally (and still are) wildly bent.

The DX125 was delivered by a forlorn Amazon delivery driver who set it carefully on my porch in a timely fashion but did not seem excited for me and was not congratulatory (based on my house cam video and images). I think after the Cricut experience I was expecting him to at least give a thumbs up and toothy grin at the camera. Alas.

It was a box in a box, thankfully there was no struggle induced by the negative air pressure box-in-a-box-a-hair-bigger, so it came out nicely and was in pristine condition as well. There was less plastic and while the mat was in a slightly curved position, it pulled out and immediately went nice and flat.

SET UP:

The Cricut came with many separate pages/booklets/leaflets in a disgusting waste of paper that all appeared to be helpful guides and how-to-start slips but none of them really were worth existing (A single sheet entirely with a link, another 3 whole sheets with once sentence on each). The Cricut Design Space program for my Windows Lenovo IdeaCentre AIO as well as the mobile app for my Samsung Note 10.1 2014 edition and Samsung Note 10+ phone all installed seamlessly. Start up and updates were very fast and instant and I was ready to go in minutes. I was expecting to have to do the "wifi/WPS button" marathon race up and down my steps to get it to work (like it always turns into when setting up a new voice controlled plug or a printer). But I sat on my butt and everything worked and talked before my eyes. I tested it first on some decorative vinyl, card stock, the minute piece of fabric included, and the drawing pen on plain paper.

The Brother set up was also easy and I was not judged by my dog during wifi set up for this one either. The built in menu is yes, limited when compared to a $1100 smart phone, but it's not laggy and is fairly easy to understand (and you can learn as you go without it freezing, changing or deleting something irreversibly, or having it shoot blades at you for your stupidity). The Canvas Workspace was also as easy to install everywhere. I tested the Brother on all the same materials.

SOFTWARE COMPARISON

First let me start by saying, I've been working on computers since I was 8 when it was a word processor with just a blinking green cursor and getting floppy disk terribly pixelated games like Snarfs and Paganitzu was believed by me and my brother to be the BEST it could ever get. I like doing everything myself, use Nova Launcher on all my phones, was satisfied with Windows XP and hated everything after, I literally cried when I had to upgrade my computer and it forced me to give up Adobe PS7 (having grown up with earlier versions and not wanting any progression beyond that one), and I despise the most popular and most dumbed-down versions of everything. So I went into this having preconceived notions of which one I'd do best with and prefer. Yes, using both so far have just solidified my preference.

Cricut Design Space is very easy, very simplified, and while it won't let you do everything you may want to do, it does force you to find your way to a conclusion. The ease of access to pre-made images and fonts and ideas is spectacular. It is the laggy-ier of the two by far and there have already been multiple times it stopped responding and I had to just close out and try again (my computer has the processing power and memory (and internet connection)to probably send a NASA shuttle up so it wasn't my system being bogged down). The way this software encapsulates and isolates everything you put into it or use is expected and annoying. Even if you want to print or view something on your computer not involving the actual program, it's virtually impossible (no luck with screen shots or anything). So while the newest version allows you offline access, it all can only be worked on in the program itself. I found one work around by using my phone's S-Pen screen write function but the image size is limited. I'm not going to really get into prices, but there's a lot of costs if a majority of your work is not from scratch. UPDATE AFTER SEVERAL WEEKS: It seems to freeze and I have to close out at least once per session but the restart speed of the program is fast at least.

Brother Canvas Workspace has apparently gone through significant changes recently and it's a nice program. It's a bit more limited in things you can do as far as editing, but it shines in using and making your own/external ideas. It's a real DRAG that it doesn't come with the scanning mat because anyone who uses this machine should be doing so because of this feature. I got one for $23 on amazon (not available in stores near me) and waited 5 long days (2 days of waiting after the machine actually arrived). I never have hit into lags and while the file format for creations is limiting, it's very easy to send it places and edit. There can be a slight delay in seeing projects that you've sent from one device to the machine and then want to view on another device but it does all work.

ACCESSORIES

It's way easier to go bonkers with the Cricut. Partly because EVERYTHING is separate but also because Cricut stuff is available everywhere. I hadn't even gotten the Maker yet and I was monitoring prices of the foil transfer tool, the QuickSwap attachments, the Deep Blade tool, scoring tools, pens etc. I picked up the basic tools set for crazy cheap (Prime vs Premo days were cricut-acular!) This tool set is fine for no matter what cutting machine you use, they're decently well made they just look like space dental instruments designed by Apple (aesthetics get a 0 but function gets a 5/5). I got some decorative vinyl on clearance (2/3 were cricut brand). Sadly, the awesomely performing fabric cutting wheel is going to collect dust in my life since I don't work much with fabrics other than fixing my own clothes and taking things in and making my dog his bed cover (preferably ONE time thing). This tool is a deal-breaker for people that cut a lot of unbacked fabric, I've yet to find evidence that the Brother cutter can 100% hold a candle to it at this point. I also ordered an off-brand wide scraper tool here on Amazon since the one included in the kit was dinky. I bought the scoring pen, the deep cut blade, a 12x24 supergrip mat and that's it. The standard mat works well, albeit will never flatten. The lavender high grip mat is quite tacky but behaves.

The Brother comes with a stylus I've still never used and a spatula thing that's mediocre. The free sheet of sparkly deep blue card stock paper was absolutely gorgeous though and worth everything in the Cricut's "to get started" envelope. The standard grip mat is absurdly sticky. I gave up after first use of ever getting some of the fuzzies, dog and human hairs and lint off of it. It also partially lifts/rips the backing of my Oracal 631 vinyl which is a major problem (I've tried every depth setting). Everything sticks to it even though I use it and immediately return it to it's plastic sleeve. After the second use it has things on it I can't get off that are causing lifts/bubbles for everything I try to put on it. My most immediate purchase was the scanning mat as mentioned above. Both mats read and feed first time every time so far. Baby wipes can help with loose lint/hair/debris after each use but more dog hair somehow floats on in the 30 seconds I allow it to dry before replacing the plastic. The low-tack mat was a god-send, it's going to be my go-to because the standard is just too tacky.

PERFORMANCE:

Both cut things crisply and equally well, other than the Brother insists on cutting through the vinyl AND backing on every setting. The Brother is much faster (both to initiate, design and actually do the work). They're both pretty quiet, the Brother is slightly quieter. The Brother's front tray is noticeably deeper which makes it nice when you don't have much of a workspace as far as depth because it's there to support the mat front and back.

UPDATE: The winner for cutting vinyl so far is the Brother. It took some tweaking beyond just selecting HALF cut, but even after multiple test runs the Cricut Maker still cuts through everything more times than not.

THOUGHTS SO FAR:

They can both do the exact same plethora of things equally well with the exception of fabrics- the Cricut fabric cutting wheel is top notch. The Cricut's mats are easier to deal with. The Brother is faster and the auto blade setting is super convenient, you don't have to select all the right things it just knows and does. I had to invest more money into getting the Cricut to accomplish the same things and if you pay for monthly/yearly access, you'll always be paying something. The Brother is perfect for me and those that draw/design their own unique stuff a majority of the time and the ability to scan in ANYTHING is incomparable. I got the Maker when it was marked down to $369 from $399. I got the Brother when it was $314, from $399.

The Cricut is sleek and shiny and modern and looks like something from Black Mirror's or Electric Dream's vision of the future. Anyone can use it and with the right tools, accessories, etc it can do anything.

The brother resembles a printer from the 80's and 90's (do I feel almost nostalgia as a grimace?), the lighter grey body color is just a few shades off of that dingy tan they all seemed to be. It doesn't have the slow unfold of the cover or the drawer and the plastic of the draws doesn't feel as sturdy and sleek. On the inside, though, it's first class. The touch screen is an unfortunate requirement, but it responds fast and is easy to figure out. Even with the learning curve, the design time and establishing settings and inputting commands and running time were ALL faster compared to using the Cricut.

I can really see why some people like one or the other, and many people have and use both. Regardless, this is going to be a fun trial period (til end of Jan 2021) to solidify the winner!

UPDATE:

This morning I put both machines to the test- I wanted business cards made of 100 lb card stock with my logo, my favorite font, etc. Both machines failed me to do everything start to finish.

The cricut would not draw my little logo image (nothing I did and no file type allowed it to be edited to be a draw object). The Brother refused to export/transfer/keep the logo image and the periods in my text because they were all too small. So I looked to my trusty canon pixma ix6820. I made the business cards in a word document. Then I put in light outlines for each and transferred it into both canvas workspace and design space. It was very easy to just make a project on the brother that cut out the rectangle outlines nearly perfectly (I also scanned the word document in and did it that way too but my initial outline color was too light (it matched the light silver of the cardstock I was using) and it didn't pick out the only thing I needed- the outer rectangle the first time. The cricut failed in that it cut out the rectangles way off from where the card outlines actually were though I had aligned to the mat grid and the on-screen grid perfectly and did a test cut which aligned normally.

Update 3/2021- the cricut is getting sold to a new home and I am very happy with the Brother. I acquired the Vinyl Auto blade kit and it works fantastically even on the sharpest and tiniest cuts. Superior to the cricut which ended up frequently snagging and clogging with some little cut out pieces. After doing more research and seeing some quilting stores test and display it's fabric cutting ability, I could presume (but without real personal evidence as I just don't cut fabric) that the Brother CAN hold a candle to the cricut.

Yes the cricuts ready to make projects were handy when I wanted to do a quick not exactly personal gift for someone but I have never and will never use that feature enough to justify the costs. Also the cricut seems wasteful to me. It spreads cuts and objects out to use up more material and when you try to move objects closer together it will sometimes do it but often ignore your placement and spread them back out (and even off the grid) when you tell it to start.

It took slightly more time to really get acquainted with all the tools and abilities with the brother but I retract my previous statement that it doesn't allow as much editing.

I am very happy with going solo with the Brother. A small amount of supplies for it are available at places like Joanns, Amazon has the rest and I've gotten excellent deals from AllBrands website which carry EVERYTHING. Yes the Brother website itself is fairly useless for purchasing most things. I almost never use anything but the light tack mat. I am very happy with longevity and function of the blades and mats and machine itself with the Brother.

rating 5.0. Works with most brands, less is more A small drop or two will recondition most clays. I've used it on Sculpey III, Premo, Fimo, Cernit. Very little is needed, a bottle will probably last most people years.

I have had 2 fimo products- a Fimo effect and a Fimo professional that needed more than a drop and it just became a mess- still crumbly but stained everything and became sticky yet still hard and unworkable. I had previously attempted to warm them them a bit and used my pasta machine and using the conditioner was my last ditch effort. I think those packets of clay were just kind of garbage-y.

rating 5.0. Great kit This is an inexpensive and great kit to have. The baking cushion is handy for all polymer clay projects, though it's meant for small and light weight pieces. I used it for a 3x3 inch 4 mm high sculpture/plaque and it permanently mushed down and deformed the pad. The blade is a great size. The needle tool works excellently with proper technique and can also in a pinch be used for texturing other projects. The sand paper are perfect grits but get used up quickly so I recommend big packets of your most used sizes. The roller is fine, I don't use it as I prefer heavier and longer acrylic ones.

The circle cutter is of very good quality and makes a crisp perfect circle with no trimming or smoothing needed- perfect border. I ended up using the pouch for other things and the guide is helpful to start you with basic information.
rating 5.0. Stainless steel, good size selection Good price and selection of sizes, well cut, nice quality. I use these for findings involving polymer clay sculptures, figures, jewelry,etc

rating 5.0. Works great with polymer clay and beads I use this in polymer clay sculpture, beads, and regular beads and jewelry. I like the length, the finish is strong, they can be bent securely and it was a good price. With polymer clay for a secure bond you must use some sort of glue like crazy glue or E6000 or there will be no secure bond, especially over time, between the baked clay and the metal.
rating 5.0. Works great with polymer clay These work very well with nice crisp round edges and no trimming needed from where metal edges come together. Nice size range, I use them all frequently.

rating 4.0. Great set (lots of extras) for polymer clay I only use about 20% of these items regularly but I use them every time and use about another 30 percent of the tools here and there. For the price it was worth it.

rating 4.0. Good price, slides a bit, mostly use to support/carry Nice grid/size, but cutting on it does lead to easy slicing of the laminated layer. It cleans easy and I mostly use it to slide under something on wax paper or regular paper and to move/carry things down to the oven to put on a baking sheet

rating 4.0. Hard to apply, may have gotten dried out product Gorgeous finish but it was so dry that none of my brushes or foam swabs would pick any up, it took so much time and effort just to apply a thin coat to a 1mm edge of polymer clay jewelry. The final product is beautiful but I don't know if this is normal for this product.

rating 5.0. Great for polymer clay The corner bumpers come off all the time and it will slide around minimally, but it's a great size, the grid is great, and it's all I use. Wipes clean with just a paper towel, sometimes a little alcohol. No scratches or wear after months of use.

rating 3.0. Easy to use, sturdy Got these for findings for polymer clay pieces. They don't bend, are sturdy, colors are fine, but they do scratch really easily. Always apply adhesives like crazy glue or E6000 as polymer clay will not bind to metal ever.

rating 5.0. Non secure case but good value and color choice Great for coloring baked polymer clay where you only need a little to apply. Nice set of lots of color options. They don't sit in case well, all mine were in place at first until I tried opening it. Has every color I'll need and although they're small, they will last a really long time for my use. Happy with price, wish there was a more secure package

rating 3.0. Good selection but didn't realize they're not stainless steel The tools are small and inexpensive but work fine for non-intensive use. When I realized the rings aren't stainless steel I had to order actual stainless steel jump rings as I don't want to sell polymer clay pieces that have hardware that is not meant to last. I do use some of the big jump rings for temporarily hanging some pieces to dry or hang up with uv resin. I use the forceps a lot.

rating 5.0. My favorite art pens I've tried many other versions/brands and these are my favorite. They have the best tip- sharp edges, controllable thickness, crisp dots/lines possible, good blending. Other tips separate and go whispy, these don't. My first set was over 15 years old and still working perfectly but I needed more colors. I highly recommend Faber-Castel, they are worth the price and it was MUCH cheaper here (half price) than in AC Moore.

rating 4.0. Really nice set, not used to such a whispy tip Set came in nice package and good quality, no dried out or sketchy pens found. I initially used Faber-Castell from AC Moore but I tend to hunker down as a homebody and avoid stores from late November until after January due  to christmas shopping madness and so when I needed new ones I found these and decided to give them a try. Pens have a nice feel, the tips are quite long  and tips a little whispy so it took some getting used to with how pressure sensitive they were as far as line thickness, but thin detail work is still possible with the right touch I just haven't mastered it yet. I think I prefer the F-C ones because I press hard and preferred the precise tips and wasn't as concerned with filling in or wide strokes being more work. Good coverage and even color. As yet unsure if I would buy again but you get a lot of pens and good colors for a very good price.

input: rating 5.0. A LUXURY THAT BECAME AN ESSENTIAL I had come into a small amount of unanticipated money and my philosophy is to use it to buy a luxury I had gotten along quite well w/o. All said they were made in Italy (all my previous Gingher shears say Mexico, and have been just fine).  The knife edge blade is scary sharp.  I use these professionally, and have found that they hold a nice edge and can be resharpened as needed for a long time.  These will last a lifetime for someone who only occasionally needs to cut with them.  Be aware that a 10" shears is a large, heavy scissors! These have a very smooth chrome handle, but if you are cutting heavy material for a while, don't be surprised by a blister or a callous.  The flip side of that is that these will cut heavy stuff that the craft scissors could never get through.

rating 5.0. Great for priming custom figures! IMAGES: My second attempt compared to my first attempt at painting a hair piece for my custom Nendoroid figure. In the first attempt, you can see how uneven and clumpy it looks, as well as the tiny cracks that appeared in the acrylic paint once it had dried. Technique makes all the difference when working with products like this! Both were painted with the same acrylic, watered down to the same consistency, and applied with the same brush strokes, yet they look vastly different just because of the primer layer beneath. I used the same primer in both attempts.

I used this to prime my custom Nendoroid figures' hair pieces. It is very thin, which is actually preferable for this type of painting. I only needed a single coat, which dried very quickly and left a nice chalky texture for painting over with watered-down acrylic.

DO NOT GLOB THIS STUFF ON, particularly if you're working with a model or figure. I made that mistake with my first figure, thinking that it should be much thicker than it is. My thought was that a thicker gesso = a smoother surface for painting, and I was very wrong in this case. I ended up with uneven clumps of primer that became wet again when they were covered over with paint, and became bubbled and cracked. My second attempt, using very short, even strokes to create a thin single layer, turned out MUCH better. The acrylic paint went over it nicely and produced a perfectly smooth finish.

Concerning art supplies, it's often the technique more than the quality of the product that determines your results. This was a nice price, and when used properly, yielded wonderful results. I have included two images, one of my first attempt, one of my second attempt, to highlight the importance of technique and priming in model painting.

rating 2.0. Thin, garbagey, but technically it's gesso. This is abjectly mediocre as a surface treatment for oil paints. Making impasto is for paint, not a base. Bleh.

Priced like crap, works like crap, still prevents oil-based pigments from soaking through to a porous surface. Yes, it's gesso. It's also reminding me that my time is more valuable than it is.

I took the thing completely apart: the tension knob was assembled incorrectly, the usb jack was broken and unusable, certain internal bits were incorrectly aligned, not only was the needle missing but so was the screw that should hold one in. input:
rating 5.0. Best Entrelac Book Yet: Includes Instructions for Technique, "Designer" Rectangles, Attractive Garments Far and away the best book yet on entrelac knitting! This is the book that I wish I'd had, when I first attempted entrelac. It has big, beautiful photos that illustrate each step in the entrelac process, and it includes detailed information on things like how to avoid holes where the triangles and rectangles are joined. It also explains how to knit backward (for increased speed), how to pick up stitches (from garter and stockinette edges), and how to knit entrelac in the round.

But it gets better. It also includes written-out pattern instructions (no charts, sad to say) for knitting fancy, non-stockinette rectangles that can be combined with plain stockinette rectangles to add interest to entrelac fabric. The "designer" rectangles incorporate lace patterns, cables, relief designs, geometric designs, colorwork, beads, felting, embroidery, and "melange" techniques such as two-color garter stripes. Each "designer" rectangle pattern is illustrated by a large full-color swatch photo. By my count, there are more than 50 patterns for different fancy rectangles.

And it gets even better. There are attractive patterns for garments that use entrelac accent panels (highly-textured entrelac fabric can be overpowering if a garment is knit entirely in entrelac). There are also patterns for pillows, socks, purses, blankets, cowls, scarves, shawls, and long mitts. The garment patterns include: (1) uptown poncho, (2) rose garden baby cardi, (3) Edwardian cardigan, (4) shades of gray vest, and (5) garden path jacket. I particularly like the baby cardigan, which has an entrelac border with embroidered roses, and the garment patterns that use stripes in combination with entrelac sections. All of the patterns are basic enough to have general appeal, without being boring. Moderately experienced knitters should have no trouble knitting the patterns, because the entrelac technique is easy to master, even though it appears complicated.

Finally, there is a brief chapter on entrelac designing that includes a discussion about figuring out gauge. I really love this book, which belongs on any serious knitter's reference shelf. Another good new book on entrelac knitting is [[ASIN:1933064196 Entree to Entrelac: The Definitive Guide from a Biased Knitter]], which has quite a bit of technical how-to information, but is primarily a collection of patterns which mostly use plain stockinette rectangles.

rating 4.0. good for the basics but.... I bought this book based on the reputation of the author and, frankly, the negative reviews of the other competing book. This is a very good overview of entrelac, with basic instructions and a really great variety of patterns to play with (and some interesting projects). But it didn't cover what I had hoped for with the "entre nous" sidebars. There are issues with actually knitting entrelac -- how to handle the edges of the project when it's not seamed (slip stitch? plain knitting?) and the picking up/intersection of one rectangle with another (avoiding holes in the knitting) and the challenges of "pick up and knit/pick up and purl" evenly. These are tacit knowledge issues that might only be shared in a class, I suppose, but a book by an experienced instructor might have been expected to cover more than the obvious. I recommend this book for those for whom entrelac is a mystery, but not those for whom the details of a really well-executed project is wanted.

rating 5.0. Phenomenal! I just received my copy in the mail.  I have taken 1 entrelac class about 2 years ago.  This book is amazing.  The detailed instruction on how to knit entrelac is worth the money.  The stitchonary on different variations of entrelac blew my mind.  I like this technique, and I am looking forward to re-teaching myself this technique and all the amazing variations.  Every knitter should have a copy of this book.

rating 5.0. best book on entrelac Over the many years I've knit (+40) I've even designed my own entrelacs. The only book I've seen before this year is one from Australia done many years ago which is now unavailable. I have bought this book and the second one that was published recently and I feel this one is indeed the best of all 3 of them. First of all Drysdale doesn't complicate the subject. Entrelacs are not difficult and also easy to design as all you need to do is knit a swatch of a few triangles and then 2 rows of rectangles.  She comes at it straight on and in an organized way so the beginner or advanced as well can immediately get into it. I don't think she puts that much emphasis on knitting backwards which I don't care for anyway. Then after the basic explanations with good photos she has 65 swatches of all types of knitting which can be done in entrelac; e.g. lace, cables, colorwork, etc.  Each swatch has a big color photo and then the instructions are given from the beginning with the starting triangles. This section is followed by the patterns which begin with a poncho where only the front has entrelac. Each project is completely written out along with the yarn called for and the gauge which is sometimes given for the pattern used and at other times for the entrelacs. I find the book inspiring as just reading it with all the swatches and projects gives me ideas to do my  own thing.  I want to make the shawl but there is just about every kind of item presented. There is one page on designing your own and one on figuring out gauge. I think this book is a classic and anyone interested in knitting should consider buying it.
rating 4.0. Must  Have ! This is A Must Have Book if you are a serious knitter.excelent details and pictures to help you as you knitt your first Amazing Entralac project...love it!

rating 5.0. Beautiful book This is a beautiful book on entrelac.  I already figured out how to do entrelac from a Knitty.com pattern so I can't say whether this book could've taught me how to master the basic technique.  I will say that it has many beautiful variations and color schemes worth looking into and trying.  There are some nice patterns too, but I would reference this book more as a stitch sampler.  It certainly takes entrelac to the next level beautifully.

rating 5.0. Lessons for the challenge Entrelac Knitting is NOT EASY, but this book opens to the door to understanding the process.  The opening section is all about technique and the varieties of patterns that can be knit in Entrelac. Instructions are very clear with both excellent descriptive text and photographs of the stages involved in Entrelac knitting.  The book concludes with beautiful photographs and patterns of projects that a knitter with minimal experience can complete Thanks to Rosemary Drysdale for this book.

rating 5.0. 10 stars! I have always wanted to learn entrelac but was so afraid. It looked so intimidating.  This book is fantastic. It gives easy to read and easy to follow step by step instructions.  I was able to do it the first time with no problems.  I am so excited and would highly recommend this book.

rating 5.0. Good Basic Entrelac Information This is a well written, basic instructional book on how to do entrelac, along with some very easy ways to make it entrelac with more.  Lace, cables, construction.  For a new to experienced knitter, that has not done much, if any, entrelac knitting, this is a fine book to have in your knitting book reference collection.

rating 5.0. Thorough, complete, and inspiring This is by far one of the best put-together knitting books I've had the pleasure of running across.  I've been intrigued by entrelac for awhile and frustrated with the lack of good information out there on it.  I bought this book almost out of desperation for something worthwhile on the subject.  This book is wonderfully layed out.  The instructional section of the book is written out step-by-step complete with color pictures and easy explanations so even the most novice knitter can follow.  Instruction is followed by an inspirational section to show you just how diverse and creative you can get with this style.  The following patterns are intriguing and beautiful.  And finally the book completes with a section on how to create your own entrelac patterns.  I loved the two inspirational sections as I rarely follow patterns and was impressed with just how much you can do with this style.  I really have not seen many other knitting books layed out so well.  Definitely a required text for anyone interested on the subject.

rating 4.0. Why? Oh, Why? This is a clearly written and beautifully illustrated book.  I am new to entrelac, but having read the general instructions, I am eager to cast on and knit with this technique, feeling reasonably confident that with the help of this author, I'll be able to master it.  However, I purchased the book in part because of its positively delicious cover.  I was prepared to knit a scarf, or perhaps a lap throw in those gorgeous shades.  Imagine, then, my disappointment that there are no instructions for creating a garment, or other item, with this rainbow of colors.  I can see that the cover illustration is basic entrelac, and I understand how to chart it myself, but I would have loved to know exactly which yarn was used and in what shades.
rating 5.0. Renee says "It's the best" I shopped around at a local yarn store and looked at several books on Entrelac.  This one gave the most useful information.  It also has great patterns!

rating 4.0. Finally Entrelac I always thought sweaters knitted to get this look would be difficult to make. This book makes it look so easy.  I can't wait to start my first project with the step by step instructions.  I have no doubt I will master it quickly and this will be the beginning of many future projects to come.

rating 5.0. Entrelac at its very best Hi! I am 82 years young and have been doing all types of knitting and needlework for many years. I have 5 ceiling to the floor bookcases all on needlework. I studied in
Copenhagen and many of U S states. This Entrelac by Rosemary Drysdale, is the very
best book about this subject. The photos are supperb using yarn and needles, so you can see how the stitches are placed. Easy to read and understand.

I have recommended this book to my Knitting Guild members.

rating 5.0. Perfect book for learning Entrelac I'd tried knitting interlace items before but the directionsalways seemed to be written for people who already knew the technique.  I had read a review on knitty.com raving about this book and it wasn't expensive so I treated myself to a new book. It's perfect. Very precise and detailed instructions and step by step pictures.  the book also has little hints, tricks, and did you know to help out.  The projects have a great variety for all knitting skill levels.  Overall A++

rating 5.0. No tears Book is well laid out with easy to follow instructions that I would call a "no tears approach" to learning Entrelac. Patterns included in the book are fun and interesting. Some basic fundamentals of knitting are needed to start, such as, picking up stitches, common decreasing and increasing. Other than that, its a matter of following directions and letting your imagination go wild. Lots of fun!

rating 5.0. If you want to learn Entrelac... Entrelac looks so fascinating and almost magical, especially when it's multi-colored. But it's actually fairly simple to do.  If you can knit, purl, increase, decrease, turn your work and pick up stitches, then you can do Entrelac. I'll admit I learned it first from a series of You Tube videos.  But then I saw this book and wanted it for the many simple and beautiful patterns to make. There are totes, hats, baby blankets and sweaters, and more.  The front of the book explains basic technique. Then follows a swatch glossary, with 49 different ways to incorporate lace, ribbing, cables, bobbles, and colorwork into Entrelac. The next section has patterns as I've already mentioned. The photos are beautiful, and from what I can tell from reading (not trying yet of course), directions are clear. This isn't just a typical book of patterns, it's a reference book that I'm glad I bought.

rating 5.0. BUY THIS BOOK!!! I am a self-taught knitter and entrelac was very interesting to me, but I could not decide between Rosemary Drysdale and the Brown cover one that is currently being offered.  Thanks to Amazon, I was able to preview both titles and Drysdale's book wins hands down!! Even if you are new to knitting, you will understand the concept from day one.  I am already working on my second project from this book and i bought it at the end of June!! This is a tool for mastering Entrelac and it will be used over and over, i guarantee it!!! Love it!!

rating 5.0. My friend really likes this book I have had an interest in knitting in this manner, I went off the other reviews on Amazon and agree with them as far as instruction being well taught. One girlfriend of mine came over and saw the book and asked me to copy three patterns out of it. Would recommend.

rating 4.0. Worth it for the Patterns and Stitches This book has a wonderful selection of patterns. The 20 patterns cover all kinds of items from clothing and accessories for adults and kids to throws and pillows. Also there are directions to many variations of entrelac, some incorporating lace or cables or other techniques, making it easy for any knitter to design a pattern or vary an existing entrelac pattern. The patterns are all written out but there are no charts

I am an experienced knitter and i've done entrelac before. I bought this book for the directions to different variations of interlace and because many of the patterns looked interesting. I don't know how this book would be for a beginner, but it has many pictures of various steps in the beginning. I was surprised that some things were not mentioned, like slipping end stitches. I was hoping for more tips.

rating 4.0. It Worked! I tried, the videos, the online instructionss,...but nothing worked like the entrelac book. rating 5.0. Entrelac: The Essential Guide to Interlace Knitting I have been knitting for years and was interested in expanding my skills. This book was very well written with easy to follow photos. I was easily able to pick up the entrelac method and even have practice pieces look polished and well done! I will continue to work through the range of patterns offered and am eager to incorporate entrelac into other sweaters, scarves, etc I am working on.

rating 5.0. Absolutely beautiful I am fairly new to knitting as I have been a crocheter my entire life.  This book is amazing at teaching step by step this method and there are plenty of picture to help you see if you have gone wrong somewhere.  I highly recommend this book to anyone interested in trying something new!
rating 4.0. looks good so far The photographs are great and the projects look interesting and fun to make. I have not yet begun a project, but the directions that I have read appear straightforward.

rating 2.0. A disappointment Only a knitter would understand, but I bought the book to learn how to do Entrelac. I recognize the tremendous amount of work that went into the book but I ended up on a YouTube video. Here is why... when you look at a photo in the book with instructions below and realize what you have knit does not look like the photo in one case your tail is not in the correct postion or is located in a different position than the photo. It means something in the directions has been skipped/omitted. In this case I did a long tail cast on and Rosemary used a knitted cast on. Hence mine tail position was different than hers. So is this important?.... I don't know.  I used 2 colors the same as the photos and when I went to do the 3rd row has no end to knit with. Missing directions to weave in ends and start with new color agan weaving in ends. Book is filled photos that do not correspond with directions or omit enough instructions.

rating 5.0. Fabulous book about Entrelac knitting This book has it all !  Clear step-by-step written instructions, lovely color photos and a helpful glossary.  Novice knitters need not be intimidated by the entrelac style of knitting as it is surprisingly easy.  The simplest blanket will look like a work of art when using this stitch.

rating 5.0. This is the best I know the basics of knitting but I wanted to try to gain more knitting skills.  My favorite teacher (my mom) is no longer with me so I choose a book.  I was hesitant to buy the book since most books are written for right handed knitters and I'm a leftie.  I ordered the book and was pleasantly surprised to find that the diagrams were easy to follow and the directions were written in short sentences which makes following so much easier.  I would recommend this book to all knitters, not only does it teach you the basics of enterlac but it shows how to do multiple variations.

rating 5.0. Beautiful book and great guide to boot What a beautiful book! The pictures are wonderful and the info is great too. I already do entrelac so I am not sure about the first time entrelac knitter.

I think a class on YouTube would be a better option. I learned from VeryPink Knits on YouTube and she has great tutorials to start one off doing entrelac and other techniques. (No, I do not have any dealings with VeryPink Knits other than being a grateful new knitter that found the video).

This book is wonderful in show variations on the stitches and also has beautiful patterns. I can't wait to try some of the stitches that are presented such as the cable stitches. If you are interested in Entrelac, then this book is for you. I love doing entrelac... It adds so much depth and texture to a knitted piece. I am excited at all the possibilities this beautiful book has opened up.

rating 5.0. Wow, I taught myself Entrelac! This book is just terrific! I owned it for awhile before I opened it and discovered that with a little bit of practice Entrelac is easy.

The author provides great, detailed photos so you can follow easily. Further there are tons of example blocks with instructions so you can make blocks with various knitted techniques.

rating 3.0. A good book with issues Unfortunately with this book, you need to assume some steps because the beginner entrelac steps were NOT crystal clear.  Its frustrating when you think the instructions are what they should be and you find typographic errors or your brain is not screwed on correctly.  Once you get pass having to look for guidance using other sources the content is ok.

rating 5.0. Well written instructions Love the color photos so you can keep up with where you are. This is a confusing application if you are new to knitting. This book makes it simple and offers patterns to help you get started.

rating 5.0. Beautiful!!! I'm very satisfied of this book. Lot of photos, clear instructions. From the basic level to beautiful models (cardigans, and so on....).
Fantastic!

rating 3.0. Tough Start but Good Finish Introductory instructions are difficult to follow.  I have done a little of this based on other directions and was more successful with the other directions.  Once I get started again, I am sure I will appreciate all the pattern variations provided.

rating 5.0. Handy Reference Book Very clear instructions and I would recommend it to anyone wanting to learn Intrelac knitting. I use this book when I need to refresh myself on steps or to learn something more concerning Intrelac knitting. Very helpful. A handy reference book.
rating 4.0. need more wish there were more detailed knitting instructions for end triangles  not clear on triangles at each end with round needles

rating 5.0. Fabulous Reference Book This book is wonderful.  I am been nto entrelac for about five years, but there were some aspects of the skill that I could not figure out but this book answered my question and opened my eyes to even more entrelac procedures.  I anyone has questions on entrelac, this is the book to get.

rating 4.0. A Good, Basic Primer It is a good, basic primer for even an advanced knitter to understand the basic construction and how to add interesting stitches into an already interesting construction technique.  There is where the book ends though.  It was not written for, nore does it give the knitter much in the way of fantastic style uses.  If yo want to take Entrelac further you must either use your own imagination or go to other resources.

rating 5.0. Beautiful Patterns and Instructions This book is well written, with several basic entrelac patterns that also incorporate lace work. There are also several project patterns to choose from. If you are wanting to learn this awesome technique, I highly recommend this book!

rating 4.0. Good basic book about the process Good basic book about the process.  The patterns for garments, however, were somewhat disappointing although the techniques for differing the kinds and colors of yarn were very good.

rating 5.0. but this provides a nice comprehensive guide for beginners and a few new techniques ... I've tried entrelac before, but this provides a nice comprehensive guide for beginners and a few new techniques for those already familiar with this type of knitting.  There are loads of pictures, a nice swatch glossary, and lots of beautiful patterns.  I highly recommend it!

rating 3.0. Backwards instructions I have two main complaints about this book. First, when she describes how to create the initial triangles, she has them leaning to the right, and every web site I found has the first row triangles leaning to the left. This might not seem like such a big deal until you get to my second concern. I found this book to really be lacking in details such as how to pick up stitches, how to finish that last triangle on the row. These are the details that can cause many headaches as you try to figure things out and, inevitably, learn the hard way how not to do them. When I couldn't figure some of these things out, I turned to the internet, and that was when I discovered that compared to most of what I found, I was knitting my entrelac "backwards".

I prefer to have a book for reference - something I can set on the table in front of me as I am learning, something I can browse through for inspiration. There are certainly many beautiful photos and ideas for projects in this book, so I am giving it three stars. However, I have not found it to be my best guide for the details of how to accomplish the techniques of knitting entrelac.

rating 5.0. Good for beginners and advanced knitters I do love this book, it has really helped me to see how I can use entrelac in a lot of different ways and a lot of different patterns.  I have been able to alter designs from other patterns now that I can understand how the basic units fit together.  I was even able to change the hat pattern I was using to better fit and still keep the design intact.  Plus there are a lot of ready to make patterns already put together in here and once you have made a couple of these, you might decide you want to design your own.  This book I think would be good for a beginner as well as an advanced knitter. Entrelac is not difficult to learn, but it does take paying attention and if you make swatches as they suggest, you will get the hang of it very quickly.

rating 5.0. Good background on the history of the art Good background on the history of the art. I liked the description and teaching method of the technique, and there were several nice patterns. One of the best books on the subject I have read.

rating 5.0. I really liked this book! I already do entrelac and got this more for the patterns, which are very nice.  If you need to learn the technique this explains it quite well.  Entrelac knitting is honestly not that difficult for a more advanced knitter, but learning it can be tricky & confusing at first as you are knitting on the bias and not normally, but this book will help you.

rating 4.0. Good step-by-step guide This book contains a step-by-step instruction on an intricate pattern style that I first saw in a prayer shawl at church. Although I have only tried it once, the instructions and suggestions were pretty clear although I believe that this is intended for an experienced knitter.

rating 5.0. The Building Blocks of Entrelac I have done a little entrelac in the past, but I still felt kind of confused. I've had this book forever and finally sat down with it to relearn... And I was up and running in no time.

I love the way it breaks down how basic entrelac works in a straightforward & logical fashion, like building blocks. It also has a lot of pictures that are really helpful.

One you absorb and master the basics, there are tons of projects and variations to keep you busy.

input: rating 4.0. Great for touch-ups! Great markers. I used the gold for the MG Phenex model and the red for my Titanium Sinanju. I haven't used the other colors yet. These markers do a good job covering up the injection marks. When the paint dries it gets a little darker and a little less reflective. Still, if you use these to cover injection marks you won't even be able to see the difference unless you inspect the model very closely.
rating 5.0. easy to use and good coverage easy to use and good coverage. Be sure to shake well or the color will end up looking a little light.

rating 5.0. Great! Fantastic! . . .just not with an airbrush Very good for new gundam builders who want to add a bit of personalization.  Unfortunately I bought an airbrush shortly after, and they are somewhat useless with an airbrush, but great as a stand alone item - only buy if you aren't considering buying an airbrush though

rating 5.0. They really make your models shine. Literally. These paint markers are great. They are excellent for small detail painting or touch ups for those kits that use metallic pieces. They aren't really suitable for large area painting. They're a great addition to any gunpla tool box.

rating 4.0. Great paint markers, the gold one is BEAUTIFUL, the silver is a little "meh" Bought as a gift for my gunpla loving boyfriend. He really likes them! But be warned, the silver is kind of weird. It doesn't look nearly as shiny and nice as the gold one in the set. It looks less metallic and more.. watery? Other than the preference for how the silver color looks on the models its being used on, the rest of them are pretty good. Especially the gold one! Looks very very shiny, almost as if it were real gold!
rating 5.0. al the colors look great on my models and was very fun to use ... al the colors look great on my models and was very fun to use i would recommend this to any gunpla fan

rating 4.0. Good for those who do not plan on buy an Airbrush in the near future. Other than it took about almost a week longer than the estimated shipping date was stated as, for me who just got into Gundam models i would point out that i'm just a newb so..
I went and did the coloring for the entire strike freedom 1/100 MG kit and from what some would expect, there were tracks here and there, so i tried to touch it up the best i could and for the most part due to my tenacity I was able to get rid of most of them and make the coloring pretty even.

But, I would suggest that these be best used for touch ups on small parts. I also, due to my inexperience with models in general, I did not marker every piece individually, I just took the arms legs and torso and marked them. I learned that doing it this way makes the joints stick and possibly break(was able to fix that with glue) so I learned to gently move parts around.

Other than that I think it's worth it. But if you plan on buy an Airbrush, I would suggest you do so and use these for touch ups only. But if you are not, then these are good. But, I leave that to your own discretion.

I don't have much money so i can't just go off and buy an airbrush and paint. So for now I'm sticking with the markers.
rating 5.0. Five Stars these are great! very shiny and pretty thick, like paint markers :3

rating 5.0. Oooh, shiny... So far, I've only used the silver/grey included here, but it works really well, and I feel that the rest will prove themselves as I get a little more practice. I originally got this for the green and blue to make a custom design on the MG Talgeese Ver. EW, so being able to test out the other colours first was a nice surprise.

rating 3.0. I wish they were all as good as the gold marker in the set These are kind of hit or miss. I wish they were all as good as the gold marker in the set, which is beautiful. The red is more of a copper and the "silver" just looks gray to my eyes. I can't comment on the other two metallic markers since I haven't used them. I'm not sure what the black "touch-up" marker is exactly for, since it's too watery to cover up anything you've already hit with one of the metallics. I use it a little bit for wide panel lines, but it's generally inferior to the smaller black Gundam panel lining marker.

rating 4.0. Nice and shiny Another great set of markers would have liked a fine tip for the panel lines instead of the spear tip. Looks nice and shiny when put on but makes sure they dry before you do any painting.

rating 5.0. Great set to customize your gunpla further. I like the sheen of these markers. The metallic yellow is not the same as the gold marker, but instead is a metallic yellow. Also, it's nice to have the extra panel line pen.

rating 5.0. These are great for model detail work I loooooove these! The color goes on solid (no transparency) unless you want to smudge it up. Once it dries, the color is on! These are great for model detail work.

rating 4.0. Long wait but good product The gold is amazing I haven't yet built a gundam that had red, blue, or green metallic parts but they'd look good with these. Only complaint is shipping time

(Basically, this means that it is pink, so keep that in mind.) The gold looks really good covering up the yellow plastic. It goes on thick and spreads well. The silver is a bit thinner and harder to get a smooth coating. I haven't used the blue and green yet but assume they will be of a similar quality.
rating 5.0. Five Stars The set was just as I expected new and unopened

rating 5.0. Effective Completely reasonable and ahead of schedule shipping, and the markers all looked pretty nice when tested. The red was a little odd but once it dried looked better than anticipated. The silver was more of a shiny grey buy definitely an improvement over standard "runner" grey Definitely recommend them if you want to give your gundam that extra finesse. rating 5.0. Simple to use and will really make your models pop All you need to use these is a steady enough hand and some q-tips, toothpicks and rubbing alcohol, if you paint outside of the desired area by mistake simply rub it away with a qtip with a light drop of alcohol on it this technique is invaluable for areas like sensors and thrusters where you're very likely to paint the rim by mistake. For small areas you can rub some of the paint onto the tip of a toothpick to reach them. The green in this set is a dead on match for the green foil sticker that is heavily used in the world of Gunpla so it's a godsend for anyone who dreads placing them, the red looked a bit too pink when I first used it but the times after that produced just the perfect shade of metallic red and it looks stunning on the thrusters of my Gundam MK II, the gold is gorgeous and works great for painting in the crotch Vs that are common on UC federation mobile suits like the RX-78 and Jegan and my tip for is that to very carefully run the marker tip over only the sculpted V and use then use a toothpick dipped lightly in alcohol to carefully clean up any mistakes you might make in the end you'll find this much easier to do than dealing with the nightmare that is the yellow V sticker. The silver is more of a gray and not very shiny but will still do wonders to the dull gray used on bladed weapons and mechanical details. The panel lining marker certainly gets the job and is as simple to use as the other markers, run it over the detail you want to panel line then run a q-tip over it to clean up excess, once you do this to bring out the details on a Gundam head you'll be hooked on panel lining. With a bit of time and effort you'll have your gunpla looking better than many pre-painted action figures on the market.

As other reviewers have mentioned not only should you take each marker around 50 times and press the tip down on a firm surface before using them for the first time you should also shake them about 30 times to get the best possible results, in the tip seems to dry press it down on a firm surface to get it wet again, also holding the tip pressed down will drain a bit of the paint out handy when you want to use a toothpick or brush. Another thing to keep in mind is to not to get stressed over mistakes as even a week after painting it can be removed with a steady rubbing of alcohol.

rating 4.0. Lots of storage, Velcro tabs bug me I bought this back in March 2020, when I was finding my temperature blanket supplies beginning to become unmanageable. I have since abandoned the blanket and instead use this container to store all of my Caron Simply Soft skeins in one place. I have many other brands of yarn sorted into those foldable cube storage bins by color family but I like this storage bag better than the bins. I can fit 6 skeins into the 3 feeder pouches and I have about 7 more in the side that had held my blanket-in-progress. I keep a stash of crochet needles in one of the side pouches and currently have nothing in the other side.

I don't think I would use it for travel. The strap isn't really comfortable for wearing for very long, so this isn't a bag I would use to keep a project with me as I go about my day, either. It's more suitable to sitting on the floor while I work on the couch. One big advantage of this set-up is that it reduces the amount of yarn my cat can see, so she can watch me crochet without getting into trouble for going after the loose yarn. Win-win and when she gets too wound up, she goes after one of her toys instead.

The biggest drawback, for me, has to do with the Velcro tabs near the feeder grommets. I keep planning to cut them off as they catch my yarn when I'm working and they really damage the Caron Simply Soft yarn. Maybe it isn't so bad on other brands. I haven't come up with a solution to replace them that would keep the ends in place or keep strands separated when working with multiple colors at one time. However, other users seem to really like the Velcro tabs, so your mileage may vary.

The flatter head design compared to other more vertically narrow heads is genius! I finish projects more quickly and neatly!

rating 2.0. Disappointed. I bought this cutting mat for cutting material. I am noticing that some fibers are getting stuck in the mat. I suspect that over time this will repeat itself until I have a very uneven mat. I haven't had it long enough to determine if these cuts will heal. There are inch measurements along one side and across the bottom, the other side and the top are in millimeters. This isn't very effective when you are trying to line up your straight edge prior to making a cut. My solution was to use a tape rule and measure from the edge of the mat to the straight edge at 2 different points. It works but it wastes time. Although listed as 48" long the measurements along that edge only go to 46" and there certainly isn't another 2" of outside border so if that is a requirement for a prospective buyer, they should consider something else. I could not in good conscience recommend this to a friend. This thing is big and it would be best if a user had a permanent location to lay it out.

Update: With respect to storage of this mat. Since I don't have a permanent location that I can leave the mat at, I have been rolling it up loosely and storing it vertically in an out of the way place. It is now cracking on the back in several spots. I don't know if it is because of how I store it or if there is something else going on.

Edit. I downgraded my rating to 2 stars. Although the fiber sticking in the cuts has not been a problem, a bigger problem has surfaced. This mat has started to crack on the backside. I store it rolled up, standing vertically. I figured rolling it up would be okay since that is how it was shipped to me. The mat is not yet 1 year old.

rating 5.0. Amazing results! I waited a loooong time to purchase these brushes, by any brand. I saw many reviews, and even try-out videos and they seemed worth the money. I finally decided to order these. The price point is great and a variety of colors in one order. I tried them out and what a difference from what I've been using. I've been using the cheap, traditional flat foam blenders that attach to a little handle. Then I switched to the dome-shaped daubers which were slightly better, but these brushes...they aren't foam, they have super fine, soft bristles that give super smooth results and don't leave a big blob in the shape of the brush when you first lay the color down. My only compaint is that this set has 2 brushes with blush toned handles that I will probably never use. I would rather have had this set include a true purple handled brush and a yellow handled one. When I looked at the picture, I didn't realize the set didn't contain these colors. The darkest handle looks purple in the photo, but it's closer to indigo, almost black. I can still use them, but it's gonna "urk" me that I didn't' get the more common-colored handles. I see that they have other sets that do include actual purple and yellow handled brushes and that's probably the reason they do it that way...to get you to purchase both sets.
I did price some other "name brand" sets from well-known crafting companies and they were waaaay more expensive than these. Still a great value for the price, and I think these will work just as well as the more expensive brands.

rating 5.0. The More the Merrier! I make cards and do a lot of ink blending and it's nice to have multiple brushes for all the colors so you don't get contamination. While I do appreciate the branded ones I can get from card suppliers, I can't afford to buy as many as I need from them. These are just as good as far as I can tell and they come faster. Some things are basics that I feel don't require the big spend.
